concepts.title: Equations of Normal
concepts.conceptBody:
Equation of Normal in Different Forms

10.1. Point Form 

The required normal is the straight line which passes through the point $\left( \text{x'},\text{y'} \right)$ and is perpendicular to the tangent, i.e., to the straight line

$y=-\frac{{{b}^{2}}{x}'}{{{a}^{2}}{y}'}x+\frac{{{b}^{2}}}{{{y}'}}$

Its equation is therefore, $y-{y}'=m\left( x-{x}' \right)$

where $m\left( -\frac{{{b}^{2}}{x}'}{{{a}^{2}}{y}'} \right)=-1$

i.e., $\text{m}=\frac{{{\text{a}}^{2}}\text{{y}'}}{{{\text{b}}^{2}}\text{{x}'}}$

The equation to the normal is therefore, $y-{y}'=\frac{{{a}^{2}}{y}'}{{{b}^{2}}{x}'}\left( x-{x}' \right)$

i.e., $\frac{x-{x}'}{\frac{{{x}'}}{{{a}^{2}}}}=\frac{y-{y}'}{\frac{{{y}'}}{{{b}^{2}}}}$

10.2. Parametric Form 

Equation to the normal at the point whose eccentric angle is $\phi $. The coordinates of the point are $\text{a}\,\text{cos}\,\phi $ and $\text{b}\,\text{sin}\,\phi $.

Hence, in the result of the last article putting

$\text{{x}'}=\,\,\text{a}\,\,\text{cos}\,\,\phi \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ and }\!\!~\!\!\text{ {y}'}=\,\,\text{b}\,\,\text{sin}\,\phi $

it becomes $\frac{\text{x}-\text{acos}\phi }{\frac{\text{cos}\phi }{\text{a}}}=\frac{\text{y}-\text{bsin}\phi }{\frac{\text{sin}\phi }{\text{b}}}$

i.e., $\frac{ax}{\text{cos }\phi }-{{a}^{2}}=\frac{by}{\text{sin}\,\phi }-{{b}^{2}}.$

The required normal is therefore

$\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ ax }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }sec\,\,\phi \,\,-\,\,by\,\,cosec\,\,\phi \,\,=\,\,{{a}^{2}}\,\,-\,\,{{b}^{2}}$

10.3. Slope Form 

The equations of the normal of slope $m$ to the ellipse $\frac{{{x}^{2}}}{{{a}^{2}}}+\frac{{{y}^{2}}}{{{b}^{2}}}=1$ are given by

$y=mx\mp \frac{m\left( {{a}^{2}}-{{b}^{2}} \right)}{\sqrt{\left( {{a}^{2}}+{{b}^{2}}{{m}^{2}} \right)}}$

at the points $\left( \pm \frac{{{\text{a}}^{2}}}{\sqrt{\left( {{\text{a}}^{2}}+{{\text{b}}^{2}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{m}}^{2}} \right)}},\pm \frac{\text{m}{{\text{b}}^{2}}}{\sqrt{\left( {{\text{a}}^{2}}+{{\text{b}}^{2}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{m}}^{2}} \right)}} \right).$

11. NORMALS FROM A GIVEN POINT 

Equation of normal at $\left( a\,\text{cos}\,\,\theta ,b\,\text{sin}\,\,\theta \right)$ to the ellipse $\frac{{{x}^{2}}}{{{a}^{2}}}+\frac{{{y}^{2}}}{{{b}^{2}}}=1$ is

$ax\sec \theta -by\operatorname{cosec}\theta ={{a}^{2}}-{{b}^{2}}$

  • $\frac{\text{ax}\left( 1+{{\tan }^{2}}\frac{\theta }{2} \right)}{\left( 1-{{\tan }^{2}}\frac{\theta }{2} \right)}-\frac{\text{by}\left( 1+{{\tan }^{2}}\frac{\theta }{2} \right)}{2\tan \frac{\theta }{2}}={{\text{a}}^{2}}-{{\text{b}}^{2}}$

⇒ Let $\tan \frac{\theta }{2}=\text{t}$

⇒ $by{{t}^{4}}+2\left( ax+{{a}^{2}}-{{b}^{2}} \right){{t}^{3}}+2\left( ax-{{a}^{2}}+{{b}^{2}} \right)t-by=0$

If this normal passes through a given point $\left( \text{h},\text{k} \right)$, then

$bk{{t}^{4}}+2\left( ah+{{a}^{2}}-{{b}^{2}} \right){{t}^{3}}+2\left( ah-{{a}^{2}}+{{b}^{2}} \right)t-bk=0$

This is a four degree equation in t which means maximum four normals can be drawn from a given point to the ellipse.

Let ${{t}_{1}},{{t}_{2}},{{t}_{3}}$ and ${{t}_{4}}$ be the roots of this equation, so that,

$\mathrm{t}_1+\mathrm{t}_2+\mathrm{t}_3+\mathrm{t}_4=-2 \frac{\mathrm{ah}+\mathrm{a}^2 \mathrm{e}^2}{\mathrm{bk}}$ ...(i)

$\Sigma \mathrm{t}_1 \mathrm{t}_2=0$ ...(ii)

$\Sigma \mathrm{t}_1 \mathrm{t}_2 \mathrm{t}_3=-2 \frac{\mathrm{ah}-\mathrm{a}^2 \mathrm{e}^2}{\mathrm{bk}}$ ...(iii)

and $\mathrm{t}_1 \mathrm{t}_2 \mathrm{t}_4=-1$ ...(iv)

we know

$\tan \left( \frac{{{\theta }_{1}}}{2}+\frac{{{\theta }_{2}}}{2}+\frac{{{\theta }_{3}}}{2}+\frac{{{\theta }_{4}}}{2} \right)=\frac{{{\text{s}}_{1}}-{{\text{s}}_{3}}}{1-{{\text{s}}_{2}}+{{\text{s}}_{4}}}=\frac{{{\text{s}}_{1}}-{{\text{s}}_{3}}}{0}=\infty \,\{from\,\,trigo\}$

∴ $\frac{{{\theta }_{1}}+{{\theta }_{2}}+{{\theta }_{3}}+{{\theta }_{4}}}{2}=\text{n}\pi +\frac{\pi }{2}$

$ $
